entry.title: Re: Migrating to SAP HANA Cloud: What Actually Gets Better (Part 1 of 2)
entry.content:
@chetan_099 

When I reference S/4HANA and HANA Cloud integration in that section, I'm referring specifically to on-premise S/4HANA scenarios where HANA Cloud is being introduced as a separate analytical or data layer alongside the existing on-premise HANA database — not as a replacement for it. In those cases, integration work (SDI, SDA, or replication flows) is genuinely required to move or federate data between the two environments.

Your point on S/4HANA Cloud Public and Private Edition is correct — both run on HANA Cloud natively as their underlying database, so there's no separate integration needed at the DB layer. The "integration" that still exists in those scenarios is more about connecting HANA Cloud to external analytical tools, SAP Datasphere, or non-SAP data sources — not between S/4 and HANA Cloud itself.

entry.title: Re: SAP Generative AI Hub: RAG on SAP Data with HANA Cloud Vector Store (Part 4 of 6)
entry.content:
@chetan_099 

Really good question and it gets at a genuine architectural choice worth thinking through carefully.

No, you don't always have to embed. Embedding + vector retrieval is the right pattern when your use case requires semantic similarity, finding conceptually related content across a large corpus where the exact field values aren't known upfront. Classic RAG scenarios: policy documents, knowledge bases, unstructured notes, transport descriptions.

For S/4HANA semi-structured data: material masters, purchase orders, cost center hierarchies — you're often better served by structured retrieval first. Query the data directly via OData or RFC, filter precisely on known fields, and pass the retrieved records as context to the model. No embedding required, no vector store overhead, and you get exact matches rather than approximate similarity.

Where RPT-1 fits into this: SAP's RPT-1 model is optimized for structured tabular SAP data — it reasons over rows and columns natively without needing the data pre-embedded. For S/4 transactional data queries where you know the structure and just need intelligent interpretation of the results, RPT-1 with direct data retrieval is genuinely more efficient than an embedding pipeline.

The pattern I'd recommend for S/4 semi-structured data: use structured retrieval (OData/RFC) to fetch the relevant records, pass them directly to RPT-1 or your chosen model as context, and reserve the embedding pipeline for genuinely unstructured content — documentation, ABAP code comments, email threads, support tickets — where semantic search adds real value over exact field matching.

Embedding everything by default is a common over-engineering trap in SAP RAG implementations. Match the retrieval pattern to the data structure.

entry.title: Header: Inconsistent with the runtime object
entry.content:
Hi All
I created a custom table and later changed a data element. After this change, errors started appearing, and I was not able to add or delete fields in the table.
then I deleted the table and tried to recreate it, but the table could not be activated properly and errors continued.
Error is: No active name tab exists for ZTEST_T1 Termination due to inconsistencies Table ZTEST_T1(Statements could not be generated) Error number in DD_DECIDE (9).
To fix this, I created a new table first and then copied it using the old table name. After running the required function module(DD_TABL_ACTM), the table was activated successfully and the SE14 database adjustment was completed.
Now, the table works at runtime. I am able to activate the table and enter data without any issues. However, in SE14 → Database Object Check, one field is still shown as inconsistent (in red). This field was already deleted earlier. Also, for this specific table name only, I am not able to add or delete fields. Copying the table works, but modifying the structure does not.
If I create the same table with a different name, everything works fine. Please advise how to permanently resolve the database object inconsistency and confirm whether it is safe to transport this object to the QA system.
Runtime object check: Consistent Please find the attached screenshot. SE14 database object check: Shows field inconsistency Please find the attached screenshot.

 


Note: SAP system is ECC and database system is DB6. (SAP_BASIS :740)

Thanks,
venkat.

entry.title: Smarter Compliance Starts Here: BTP Compliance Manager Meets SAP Document Reporting & Compliance
entry.content:
Smarter Compliance Starts Here: BTP Compliance Manager Meets SAP Document Reporting & Compliance 

Crimson Partners' BTP Compliance Manager ingests any data format, leverages API connections and/or SAP Event Mesh, and delivers external documents seamlessly into SAP Document Reporting & Compliance — so your team can leverage SAP Document Reporting & Compliance as your sole E-invoicing and statutory reporting solution for all your ERP systems, SAP and non-SAP. 

Why this matters for your compliance team 

  • Faster submission cycles — Real-time and batch ingestion replace manual file handling, cutting preparation time significantly. 
  • Reduced manual intervention — Automated mapping and validation means fewer errors reaching SAP Document Reporting & Compliance and fewer corrections after submission. 
  • Multi-source flexibility — Consolidate data from subsidiaries, legacy systems, and cloud platforms without a custom integration project for each. 
  • Audit-ready by design — Every transformation step is logged, giving you a complete lineage from source record to SAP Document Reporting & Compliance submission. 

SAP-native and secure — Built on BTP, the solution inherits SAP's enterprise security, identity management, and governance controls.

Consume any format, from anywhere 

Most compliance platforms are built around a single, rigid data model. If your source data doesn't conform, you spend weeks on transformation projects before a single report is filed. The BTP Compliance Manager takes a fundamentally different approach. 

Whether your finance team exports Excel workbooks, your ERP streams XML, or a subsidiary drops flat CSV files onto a shared drive — the platform accepts them all, natively. 

No pre-processing. No brittle middleware to maintain. The ingestion layer handles format detection and validation automatically, flagging anomalies before they reach your compliance pipeline. 

Event-driven at its core: SAP Event Mesh integration 

Static file uploads are only part of the story. For organisations running on SAP S/4HANA, SAP BTP services, or any event-producing system, the BTP Compliance Manager connects natively to SAP Event Mesh — the enterprise messaging backbone of the SAP Business Technology Platform. 

This event-driven architecture unlocks near-real-time compliance visibility. Compliance teams no longer need to wait for overnight jobs or month-end extracts. The data arrives as it's created in the sources systems, verified, and routed automatically. 

Intelligent transformation into SAP Document Reporting & Compliance 

Ingesting raw data is step one. What happens next is where the real value is delivered. The BTP Compliance Manager applies a configurable transformation engine that maps incoming data — whatever its original structure — to the exact schema required by SAP Document and Reporting Compliance. 

END-TO-END DATA JOURNEY 

* Available as on-demand integration. Scoped per client requirements. 

The transformation layer handles field mapping, data enrichment, currency conversions, date normalisation, and validation against SAP Document Reporting & Compliance business rules — all before a single record reaches the reporting engine. Mapping templates can be configured per entity, legal jurisdiction, or data source, giving compliance teams granular control without requiring developer involvement for every change. 

Error handling is built in throughout. Records that fail validation are quarantined, logged with a clear reason code, and surfaced in a review queue in the Compliance Manager — meaning your SAP Document Reporting & Compliance submissions always contain clean, verified data. 

The compliance pipeline 

Every document goes through the same five stages. Each is a hard gate. A document that does not pass goes no further, and the submitter gets a clear explanation of what failed.

By the time a document reaches SAP Document Reporting & Compliance it has passed security screening, format conversion, schema validation, and country-specific business rule checks. What arrives is a verified, compliant external document. 

Custom transformation layer 

No two enterprise landscapes look the same. Source systems across different ERP platforms, procurement tools, and subsidiaries each produce invoice data in their own format. Expecting every system to conform before onboarding is where most compliance projects stall. 

 

The Crimson Compliance Manager includes a custom transformation layer built to adapt to each client’s existing setup. When a document arrives in a source format that differs from the e-invoicing standard for that country, the platform converts it automatically before validation runs. Transformation is configured per country and per client. Support for new formats can be added on demand, with no changes to the core application.

Technical architecture, solution for AR and AP 

External documents from ERP systems can be connected to the Compliance Manager via API/Event Mesh (for high volumes) or CSV/Excel uploads (for low volumes) to pass the data for billing documents/E-reports to SAP Document Reporting & Compliance. From there, the client reviews and submits to the relevant tax authority through the standard SAP Document Reporting & Compliance workflow. 

The platform handles SAP communication securely through BTP’s connectivity layer. Authentication is managed automatically. The process from submission to SAP Document Reporting & Compliance delivery runs without manual intervention once a country is configured. 

For countries such as Saudi Arabia and Oman where B2C E-invoicing is required, the POS systems can be connected via APIs to the Compliance Manager to pass the simplified customer invoice data to SAP Document Reporting & Compliance (for simplified and robust integration) for E-invoicing automatically and in real-time/batches. 

For countries with E-reporting requirements such as France, finance data required for E-reporting can be extracted from source systems using the same methods and converted to the data format for SAP Document Reporting & Compliance to generate and submit the E-reports. 

For vendor invoices and AP processes in non-SAP systems, the E-invoices received in the SAP Document Reporting & Compliance will be passed back to the ERP systems via Compliance Manager, which will convert the data to the format required by the ERP/Vendor Invoice Management systems and carry out 3-way matching if required. SAP Document Reporting & Compliance is not where the vendor invoices will be processed or rejected, but rather a conduit to receive vendor E-invoices. The vendor invoice processing will continue to be carried out in the ERP/Vendor Invoice Management systems or in BTP. 

Deployment options, SaaS or On-Premise 

The Crimson Compliance Manager is available in two deployment models depending on your organisation’s infrastructure preferences and data requirements. 

What this means in practice 

Built for enterprise, ready for what comes next 

The Crimson Compliance Manager is built for organisations managing compliance across multiple countries, entities, and source systems. A single pipeline handles the full journey from document intake to SAP Document Reporting & Compliance delivery, with country rules and transformation settings managed centrally. When a new market goes live or a regulation changes, the platform adapts without disrupting what is already working. 

As E-invoicing networks continue to expand across more markets, the platform is designed to grow with them. The same compliance pipeline that handles direct integrations today is built to accommodate broader network connectivity as those requirements evolve. 

Do check out the blog post here :- https://crimson-partners.com/smarter-compliance-starts-here-btp-compliance-manager-meets-sap-document-reporting-compliance/

entry.title: Re: Cannot create Custom Fields for Material Document Hea...
entry.content:
Hi LBONAR22,

This questioned sounded familiar, in doing some research, seems I answered it last year 🙂 

Add Custom Field in Material Document Header Level

In that answer, I suggested to the OP to file an influence request.

We also suggested to consider another object like Journal Entry, but in your case, maybe the delivery or delivery item?

In your case, could you make use of the Material Item and create your fields there.

How many Material Items do you typically have on the Material Doc? if you have typically 1, there is not much difference from having the field defined on the header.

You can use the BADI "MMIM_ITEM_PROVIDE_DATA" to update the custom fields. It also seems to have already the fields you need to reference (reference and header fields, etc.) to pull your data.

You want to modify the MMIM_GR_LABEL_E form correct? That form has the Item node, and the Custom Field can already populate there.

I hope this helps

Thank you

Jerry

entry.title: The Challenges You Face and How SAP Business Data Cloud Helps
entry.content:
Data stewards are accountable for data quality, compliance, and trust, but operate across fragmented systems, manual processes, and growing regulatory pressure—often without clear ownership or visibility.  For data stewards, SAP Business Data Cloud is not “more governance tooling.” It is a way to operationalize governance, scale trust, and turn stewardship into a measurable business enabler.

This is where SAP Business Data Cloud changes the conversation. It gives data stewards a governed, business‑ready foundation where business context, semantics, data, and usage all come together. Governance isn’t separate from analytics—it’s embedded in how data is shared and consumed.

Let’s talk a bit about problems and solutions:\

Fragmented data and inconsistent definitions

Your challenge:
Key business data is spread across SAP and non‑SAP systems, often defined differently by different teams. This makes it hard to enforce standards or build confidence in reporting and analytics.

How SAP Business Data Cloud helps:

  • Brings business data together with a consistent semantic layer
  • Helps align technical data with shared business definitions
  • Makes governed, trusted data easier to discover and use across analytics and AI scenarios

What this means for you:
Less time reconciling definitions. More confidence that people are working from the same understanding of the data.

Manual governance and limited visibility

Your challenge:
Governance work often lives in documents, spreadsheets, or disconnected tools, making it hard to track ownership, issues, and progress.

How SAP Business Data Cloud helps:

  • Connects governance concepts directly to live data assets and processes
  • Improves visibility into where data comes from, how it’s used, and who is responsible
  • Supports more consistent, repeatable governance practices instead of one‑off fixes

What this means for you:
You can focus on managing and improving data, not chasing information or enforcing rules manually.

Compliance pressure without operational support

Your challenge:
You’re expected to support regulatory and internal compliance, but controls are often indirect or hard to demonstrate during audits.

How SAP Business Data Cloud helps:

  • Enables clearer lineage, transparency, and traceability across data flows
  • Helps connect governance policies to actual data usage
  • Makes it easier to show how data is managed, protected, and governed in practice

What this means for you:
Stronger audit readiness and reduced risk—without slowing down the business.

Building trust with business and IT stakeholders

Your challenge:
Business users want speed. IT teams want stability. You need both to trust the data and the governance behind it.

How SAP Business Data Cloud helps:

  • Provides a shared foundation where business and technical teams work from the same governed data
  • Supports analytics and AI use cases without bypassing governance
  • Helps you communicate the value of governance in terms business leaders understand

What this means for you:
You gain credibility as a trusted partner, not a blocker—strengthening your influence in the buying and decision‑making process.

Again, it’s not about “more governance.” It’s about giving data stewards the structure, transparency, and leverage they need to protect and elevate the value of enterprise data.

Want to learn more about SAP Business Data Cloud as it applies to your role?  Check out this guide: One Foundation. Every Role. Smarter Insights.

entry.title: Re: SAP Document AI – Quality Certificate processed succe...
entry.content:
Hello!

 

SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition does support automatic processing of supplier quality certificates with SAP Document AI. When everything is aligned correctly, the system can:

-                Automatically recognize the quality certificate

-                Match it to the existing Certificate Receipt

-                Attach the document

-                Automatically change the status from “Expected” to “Received”

 

In the current flow, the certificate PDF was uploaded directly in the SAP Document AI portal using a generic (custom) document type and an OCR‑only schema.

This means:

-                Document AI successfully performed OCR and processing

-                SAP S/4HANA QM was not involved in the upload process

-                The system therefore had no trigger to link the document to the Certificate Receipt

As a result, the Certificate Receipt correctly stays in “Expected” status and the document is not attached automatically.

 

For automatic status update and attachment to work, SAP requires the document to be uploaded from within SAP S/4HANA, not directly in the Document AI tenant.

The intended process is below (details in this blog post: https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-blog-posts-by-sap/smarter-quality-management-with-ai-assisted-processing-sap-s-4hana-cloud/ba-p/14184266😞

  1. The certificate receipt is created automatically at Goods Receipt (status = Expected)
  2. The quality certificate is uploaded using the SAP app “Upload Quality Certificate Documents” (app ID: F8858)
  1. SAP then sends the document to Document AI for data extraction
  2. SAP uses the extracted data (PO, item, material, supplier, etc.) to find a match
  3. If a match is found the document is attached and the certificate receipt is automatically set to “Received”

 

If the match cannot be determined clearly, SAP switches to a manual review process.

If automatic matching does not happen (for example, if data is missing or unclear), SAP expects manually to:

-                Review the document

-                Attach it manually if needed

-                Change the certificate status to Received in Manage Certificate Receipts

 

About the “Upload Quality Certificate Documents” it becomes available automatically when:

-                Scope item 7TC (Integration with SAP Document AI) is active

-                The business catalog QM – Incoming Quality Certificates is assigned

-                The user role is refreshed

entry.title: Introduction to Next-Gen SAP Ariba
entry.content:
Introduction


Next-gen SAP Ariba marks a foundational transformation of SAP's procurement platform. It is the first modern, AI-native source-to-pay suite designed for the era of intelligent business operations. This comprehensive modernization, launched with the February 2026 (2602) release, transforms procurement from reactive to proactive, manual to autonomous, and siloed to orchestrated across the entire business ecosystem.
Next-gen SAP Ariba is built on SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) using a modern, cloud-native architecture with API-first integration patterns.

Business Benefits:
Procurement Excellence: Streamlined end-to-end processes from strategic sourcing through payment with real-time supplier engagement and advanced integration capabilities
Enhanced Compliance: Guided buying enforces company policies with proactive warnings, automated validation, and comprehensive audit trails
Supplier Collaboration: SAP Business Network facilitates global buyer-supplier connections, supporting ethical supply chain development and transaction efficiency

entry.title: Exploring SQL Console Features in SAP HANA Cloud: Lessons from My Internship!
entry.content:
Hi everyone!

My name is Joshua and I have been a Product Management Intern for the past four months. I had the opportunity to explore a wide range of features and tools used in SAP HANA Cloud Central. During this time, I became particularly interested in SQL tooling and how modern platforms are evolving to make database development more accessible and efficient.

In this post, I want to highlight a few SQL-focused features that stood out to me and that I think are worth exploring further!

The first feature I wanted to highlight is the Gen AI capabilities within the SQL Console in SAP HANA Cloud. As the industry continues to embrace AI-powered tools, it is interesting to see how SAP is applying these capabilities to SQL for development and query creation.

Within the SQL Console, there are three new AI-assisted options: “Run Prompt”, “Explain SQL Statement using AI”, and “Optimize SQL Statement using AI”. Run Prompt allows users to convert natural language into SQL statements, which is especially helpful for new SQL users who may not yet be familiar with syntax. Explain SQL Statement using AI uses AI to translate SQL queries into natural language, making it easier to understand what a query is doing, particularly for those less experienced with SQL. Finally, Optimize SQL Statement using AI helps improve existing queries by suggesting more efficient and readable versions.

Another feature that stood out to me is code completion within the SQL Console. As you type, the console suggests SQL snippets and schema objects in real time. I’ve found this especially helpful when I’m unsure of the exact spelling of schema objects, as it allows me to quickly autofill them without breaking my workflow. It has also made my SQL writing more efficient by reducing the need to manually type out repetitive or complex statements. Overall, it’s a simple addition that makes working with the SQL Console more fluid and less disruptive.

The SQL Console has also recently introduced the ability to restore closed tabs. This is a feature I found particularly useful, as there have been many times when I’ve accidentally closed the wrong tab and lost my SQL queries. In those moments, having to recreate the work can be frustrating and time-consuming. With this new functionality, it’s now possible to quickly restore recently closed tabs, which makes the overall experience much more forgiving and user-friendly. The option can be accessed through the three-dot overflow menu in the top-right corner of the toolbar, and it allows you to restore up to the last 10 tabs within your current session. Overall, this is a small but impactful addition that helps prevent unnecessary rework and makes it easier to recover from simple mistakes. It’s definitely a feature I recommend trying out!

Another feature that I found particularly interesting is the ability to run SQL statements in the background within the SQL Console. This is a capability that previously existed in the Database Explorer, and it has now been brought into the SQL Console experience as well. It’s a feature that can be easy to overlook but becomes especially valuable when working with long-running queries.

With this functionality, users can execute long-running SQL statements independently of their browser session. This means that even if you navigate away, disconnect, or close the tab, the query will continue running uninterrupted. You can then return later to review and download the results once execution is complete.

In addition, the SQL Console introduces a dedicated tab in the results area for background activities. This provides a centralized view where users can monitor the status of ongoing executions, check progress, and access completed results all within the same interface.

Overall, this feature improves the handling of resource-intensive queries by removing the need to remain actively connected while they run. It creates a more flexible workflow and makes it much easier to manage long-running database operations.

The last SQL Console feature I want to highlight is the syntax help button in the toolbar. This is one I found really useful when working through unfamiliar queries, as it brings helpful reference information directly into the console.

The first dropdown, “SQL Statements,” shows the correct structure for the SQL statement you are currently working on. I found this especially helpful when I wasn’t completely sure about the required syntax or formatting. Being able to quickly check the structure and even navigate to additional documentation from within the dropdown made it much easier to learn as I went.

The second dropdown, “Tables and Views,” displays details for the objects referenced in your current SQL statement. I really liked using this to quickly verify which tables or views I was working with, especially when dealing with unfamiliar schemas. It also made it easier to check object names or spelling without leaving the console. The ability to search and insert objects directly, or navigate to them in the Database Objects app, helped streamline my workflow quite a bit.

The third dropdown, “Procedures and Functions,” offers similar functionality for procedures and functions used in your SQL statement. I found it helpful to quickly view parameters and jump directly to the relevant object or documentation for more details.

Overall, exploring these features has given me a much better understanding of how SQL tooling is evolving to become more intuitive and user-friendly. From AI-assisted query generation to quality-of-life improvements within the SQL Console, each of these additions plays a role in making database development more accessible and efficient. 

Throughout my internship, I’ve really enjoyed getting hands-on experience with these tools and seeing how even small enhancements can make a big difference in day-to-day workflows. I’d definitely recommend trying out these features for yourself, as they can meaningfully improve both productivity and the overall development experience once you start using them regularly! 

I’d also love to hear from others, what’s your favorite SQL Console feature, and are there any capabilities you’d like to see added in the future?

entry.title: S4 Post Upgrade - Profile Generator (SU25) Options
entry.content:
Hi All ,

While performing the security upgrade (SU25) as part of the S/4HANA system migration from S/4HANA 2022 SP03 to S/4HANA 2025 SP01, we observed that several new options were introduced in SU25. The impact of these changes is not clearly explained in the SAP documentation available to us. As a result, many of our roles are being affected—new authorization objects are being introduced, some existing ones are being removed automatically, and custom SU24 updates appear to be overwritten.

Before proceeding further, we would appreciate guidance on the impact of each selection under SU25 steps 2a, 2b, 2c, 2d, etc. If any detailed documentation or guidance is available, please share it.

As a precaution, we are also considering taking a User Master export post-upgrade, but before executing SU25, along with a backup of the SU24 customizing tables (USOBT_C and USOBX_C). We would like confirmation on whether these backups can be used to restore the system to its pre-SU25 state if required.

entry.title: First Half 2026 Release: What's New in SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central?
entry.content:
As organizations continue to navigate evolving workforce expectations and increasingly complex regulatory environments, HR teams need a strong and reliable core that keeps people processes moving while maintaining data accuracy and compliance. With the First Half 2026 release, we are introducing new innovations in SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central that help reduce friction in daily HR operations, improve data quality, and support scalable global HR practices.

Alongside foundational core HR enhancements, this release introduces AI agents in SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central. These agents are designed to help monitor workflows and data, surface potential issues, and support faster resolution. Let’s take a closer look at the innovations coming with this release.

Faster, More Informed Approvals with Enhanced Workflow Notifications

Approvals are a critical moment in core HR processes, yet approvers often lack the full context needed to act quickly and confidently. This release enhances workflow notifications by bringing pending, to‑be‑approved employee data directly into the notification itself. Instead of navigating into the system to review details, approvers receive the information they need upfront.

Using field mapping, administrators can now define variables for these notifications to include pending (to-be-approved) data, providing approvers with the key context they need directly in email. This added transparency reduces back‑and‑forth, accelerates decision‑making, and helps keep HR processes moving smoothly, particularly in fast‑paced or high‑volume environments.

Keeping Workflows Moving with the Pending Workflow Agent

Even with improved notifications, workflows can still stall when requests go unnoticed or approvers are unavailable. This release introduces the Pending Workflow Agent, which is designed to surface workflow status and help keep tasks on track by proactively identifying and addressing bottlenecks.

The Pending Workflow Agent monitors how long requests have been pending and evaluates approver availability to determine when action is needed. It can nudge approvers or reroute requests to delegates or higher‑level managers to avoid delays. This capability is designed to help reduce time‑to‑resolution by up to 40 percent, while minimizing administrative effort and lowering compliance risk.

                                                                          Pending Workflow Agent 

Improving Data Accuracy with the Employee Data Integration Agent

Accurate and consistent employee data is essential, particularly when supporting payroll and other downstream processes. A new Employee Data Integration Agent enables organizations to detect data inconsistencies across core HR and payroll systems before they create downstream impact.

The agent identifies discrepancies, proposes fixes, and applies approved changes to help keep employee records accurate and aligned across systems. By proactively addressing data issues, organizations can reduce manual rework, avoid payroll disruptions, and maintain a more reliable system of record.

Check out this demo to see the agent in action: Demo: Data Integration Agent

             Data Integration Agent

Simplifying Global Compliance with Country‑ and Region‑Specific Dependents Management

Managing dependents data across countries requires balancing global consistency with local legal requirements. This release introduces enhancements to dependents management to simplify updates for employees and admins while supporting compliance.

Organizations can define country‑ or region‑specific dependent types, objects, and required data fields to meet local legal requirements. Out‑of‑the‑box country settings enable faster implementation, while configuration can be easily modified through a single, unified user interface. By eliminating the need for complicated business rules, HR teams can maintain compliant dependent data more efficiently, while employees benefit from a clearer and more consistent self‑service experience. Furthermore, optimized data management ensures accurate information flows to downstream processes including payroll, benefits, and time management. 

                 Dependents management enhancements

Strengthening the Intelligent HR Core

With these delivered innovations in the 1H 2026 release, we continue to strengthen SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central as the intelligent HR core. Enhancements to approvals, the introduction of AI agents to help monitor workflows and data, and simplified localized compliance all enable more efficient operations today and establish a strong foundation for connected core HR, payroll, and talent processes across SAP SuccessFactors HCM.

Get Started with the 1H 2026 Release

  • Register for the First Half 2026 Customer Release Q&A session for Employee Central to hear directly from SAP experts
  • Explore the What's New Viewer for a complete overview of all SAP SuccessFactors First Half 2026 innovations
  • Visit the Product Release & Road Map Information page to access additional resources, including release assets and product road maps

entry.title: Re: Partial Payment using JournalEntryBulkCreateRequest API
entry.content:
Hello!

 

The error message "Document or line item does not exist for invoice reference" is particularly relevant in the context of partial payments or when dealing with credit memos and subsequent invoices.

Understand the Context: The Invoice Reference field (BSEG-REBZG) is used for line items that are related to another line item. Ensure that you are referencing the correct document number.

Verify that the Invoice Reference you are trying to enter corresponds to an existing document. If the field is empty or does not contain a valid document number, the system will trigger the error.

Ensure that the line item you are referencing is indeed related to the invoice you are processing. This includes checking for credit memos that refer to a specific invoice item or partial payments.

 

In the JournalEntryBulkCreateRequest API, the InvoiceReferenceDocument field should contain only the accounting document number. Company code and fiscal year must be provided in their dedicated fields. If these values are concatenated into the InvoiceReferenceDocument field, SAP cannot resolve the reference and returns the error “Document or line item does not exist for invoice reference.”

  1. Ensure InvoiceReferenceDocument contains only the invoice number
  2. Ensure fiscal year and company code are passed in their own fields
  3. Ensure InvoiceReferenceDocumentItem refers to the customer open item line

 

For example, correct vs. incorrect usage:

Correct structure (API‑compliant):

InvoiceReferenceDocument = "0090000169"   (invoice number only)

InvoiceReferenceDocumentFiscalYear = "2026"

CompanyCode = "BR01"

InvoiceReferenceDocumentItem = "002"

 

Incorrect structure (common cause of the error)

InvoiceReferenceDocument = "190000123410002024"

InvoiceReferenceDocument = "1900001234_1000_2024"

 

Even when all reference fields exist, SAP performs strict line‑item‑level validation for partial payments:

  1. The referenced line item must be the customer receivable open item (not a revenue or tax line).
  2. The line item must still be open (not cleared or reversed).
  3. The line item number must be in internal SAP format (including leading zeros).
  4. The reference must be applied on the customer line of the payment journal entry, not on a G/L line.
  5. The fiscal year must be the accounting fiscal year, not the document or posting date year.

If any of these checks fail, SAP returns the same error message.

entry.title: CAP Developers - Call to Action to mitigate and Apply Solution provided in SAP Note 3747787
entry.content:
On April 29, 2026, four malicious open-source package versions were distributed into the NPM ecosystem. These malicious versions appear to exfiltrate information, such as credentials, and attempt to propagate into downstream software packages as well as adjacent software repositories when installed on a system.

If you are uncertain whether your systems have been affected, it is crucial to act promptly. Begin by following the mitigation steps outlined to maintain your environment’s security. Promptly taking these actions will help protect your systems and data from potential risks.

Other Terms

  • MBT
  • NPM
  • CAP
  • SAP Cloud Application Programming Model
  • MTA Build Tool

List of compromised NPM package versions:

  • · @cap-js/sqlite - v2.2.2
  • · @cap-js/postgres - v2.2.2
  • · @cap-js/db-service - v2.10.1
  • · mbt@1.2.48

Solution

If you have identified that you may be affected, perform the following measures via provided SAP Note below:

https://me.sap.com/notes/0003747787

SAP Support Ticket Component : BC-XS-CDX-NJS

Potential references:

  • https://www.aikido.dev/blog/mini-shai-hulud-has-appeared
  • https://www.stepsecurity.io/blog/a-mini-shai-hulud-has-appeared
  • https://www.mend.io/blog/shai-hulud-sap-cap-supply-chain-attack-claude-code/
  • https://onapsis.com/blog/sap-cap-mini-shai-hulud-supply-chain-attack/
  • https://snyk.io/de/blog/bun-based-stealer-hits-sap-cap-js-mbt-npm-packages/

entry.title: Total number of records for complex unmanaged query in RAP
entry.content:
In most of the examples from SAP (https://help.sap.com/docs/ABAP_PLATFORM_NEW/fc4c71aa50014fd1b43721701471913d/254f68c7d5374e94a88241b7ce3bd414.html), the handling of total record count when requested is quite straightforward since there is only a single table involved.

But there maybe some complex reporting scenarios where it is not practical to calculate the total since it will affect the performance. How do you usually handle the total calculation? Do you give a rough estimate of the total number of records or do you really calculate the total whenever it is requested?

The issue with providing a rough estimate is that the value that you pass will usually be reflected at the table header level.

entry.title: Re: Sales Organization address fields in in Email Templates
entry.content:
Hello!

The fields related to sales organization address can be found in the following path:

  • Sales Area > Sales Organization > Address

In this node, the following fields can be accessed:

  • _SalesArea._SalesOrganization._Address.BusinessPartnerName1
  • _SalesArea._SalesOrganization._Address.BusinessPartnerName2
  • ...

You can also use the search for finding fields you'd like to add in your email template, there are over 10.000 fields available to be used in email templates.

Kind regards.

entry.title: Re: How can S/4HANA Cloud(Public) connect to non-SAP syst...
entry.content:
Hi SAPERPNewbie,

Yes, I believe the Cloud Connector is the correct way to go.

Important to know that the Cloud Connector is installed inside your network and creates a tunnel that can connect to SAP Cloud solutions.

SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition doesn't run on BTP, it is a separate solution, so I don't expect you would find a subaccount for S/4HANA Cloud. You would need to set up destinations and a comm arrangement ( SAP_COM_0574) in S/4HANA Cloud. (see the help below)

Here is the Help for Integrating OnPrem systems (including Non-SAP) https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0f69f8fb28ac4bf48d2b57b9637e81fa/82c9f05663594e5db8f923f6f88ae9bd.html?locale=en-US&version=LATEST

and a couple good blogs about the Cloud Connector

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/a-complete-guide-to-sap-cloud-connector-concepts-system-requirements-and/ba-p/14283064

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/cloud-connector-explained-in-simple-terms/ba-p/13547036

Could you let us know what type of outbound communication are you interested in? For example event notifications via the Event Mesh (in BTP) or are you looking for Data Replication, etc.?

Thank you
Jerry

entry.title: S/4HANA CDS View Dependency Navigator
entry.content:
If you need S/4HANA data for your analytical project, or you want to understand S/4HANA logic—whatever your S/4HANA information needs might be—you will need CDS views. But how do you find CDS views? You can use the Virtual Data Model and CDS Views page, which provides some information, or simply use my CDS View Dependency Navigator for a more flexible navigation across all CDS views. If this is what you need, then my new blog is for you.

You start your navigation from a selection screen on which you specify a starting CDS view or DDIC table and the navigation direction:

To navigate along the hierarchy and display source code, you click on the desired CDS view/DDIC table in the Name and Source Code columns, respectively. If at any step of your CDS view dependency navigation you take a wrong turn, no problem—just press the F3 Back button to go back. 

As you can see, the Dependency Navigator displays:

  • Dependencies
  • Navigation Path
  • Source Code
  • Important annotations such as Description, Lifecycle Status, Successor, etc.

With the Navigation Path, you will never get lost or lose what you have found. This is how to read the Navigation Path:

  • CDS views up the hierarchy are on your right, and CDS views/DDIC tables down the hierarchy are on your left.
  • The ← and → arrows indicate the navigation direction.

If the end of the CDS view dependency hierarchy is reached, an informational message will be displayed:

If you navigate down the dependency hierarchy and need to see the table structure, it can be done as well:

What else is worth mentioning is that the Navigator’s dependency scope is limited to only the CDS views/DDIC tables used in the selection and only one level up/down, without any associations. This eliminates unnecessary clutter and makes navigation easier. Compare the ADT Dependency Analyzer with the Dependency Navigator for the P_SalesAnalytics CDS view:

I have to acknowledge that the S/4HANA CDS View Dependency Navigator was inspired by the ABAP CDS View Object Flow Program. With the introduction of CDS entities in S/4HANA 2020 (ABAP 7.55), it became obsolete due to missing information about CDS entities. This is because the ABAP CDS View Object Flow Program relied on CDS view DDIC views (the sqlViewName annotation) to navigate along the CDS view hierarchy, which is missing for CDS entities.

I had to rethink the approach to finding dependencies to support both CDS views and CDS entities. The S/4HANA CDS View Dependency Navigator parses CDS view code. All it takes is some knowledge of CDS view syntax and regular expressions (regex). The only complication I encountered when parsing CDS view annotations is the mix of structured and flattened syntax, but this can be addressed by processing all combinations.

Now you know what to do if you need to add more CDS view annotation attributes to the Dependency Navigator. Here is the Dependency Navigator code—enjoy

entry.title: BiWhy Jobs – Make Jobs Work for You, Not Against You
entry.content:
Disclaimer: BiWhy is available again for FREE to everyone on biwhy.net

User load usually builds gradually. If something goes wrong, users report it quickly – either as errors or slow refreshes.

Background job load is different. It may stay unnoticed for a long time while quietly consuming CPU, memory, and database resources.

Typical job problems:

  • too many jobs hitting the system at once
  • long-running data dumps with little real value – the output is too large to open or too slow to use
  • scheduled reports that fail after wasting resources

At the same time, jobs are extremely valuable when managed well. Some companies run reporting workloads 24/7 and save end users tons of time.

BiWhy now provides analytics for Future, Running, and History jobs.

Future

Analytics show when load will hit you – by day, day of week, day of month, or hour – and who is generating it, by user, report, or project.

Running

Analytics provide the usual BiWhy view of currently running jobs.

History

Analytics show failure patterns over time – including jobs that never finish successfully, or jobs above a failure rate you define.

 

In one recent case, I found 611 reports that had never finished successfully in the first four months of 2026.

Together, they accounted for about 7,130 failed instances and 172 days of processing time. The real number is 2–3 times higher, since the system keeps only the last 100 instances.

About 50 of those reports ran daily or even more often. Around 100 had an average runtime above 30 minutes – most in hours, some in days.

And that is only elapsed processing time. In real compute terms, the cost is much higher: those jobs were burning many CPUs in parallel, wasting years of compute and producing exactly ZERO useful outcome.
Some of them were also bringing down BOBJ nodes almost daily.

Practical cleanup tips:

  • 100% of sibling instances failing – likely a report issue
  • Some sibling instances succeed while others fail – likely a schedule-specific issue, such as overly broad prompt values
  • Frequently running reports – more executions, more waste
  • Long-running reports – fewer executions, but higher cost per failure

Tidy up your jobs. Spread them properly. Remove the ones nobody needs. Fix the ones that frequently fail.

Use BiWhy to make jobs work for you, not against you.

Future jobs load by time

Failing jobs table

entry.title: SAP DRC Configuration
entry.content:
Hello Community,

Looking for SAP DRC Configuration and process.

Anyone can brief it like functional SD and FI configuration and BTP configuration.

Means a lot

I have analyzed few things related to this sharing with you all but still configuration steps and BTO configuration steps required.

Bullet Points 

SAP DRC Definition And Usage

SAP DRC High Level Architecture 

Supported Compliance Tasks by Country or Region

KSA Supported Scenarios

SAP DRC Cloud Edition Activation Steps

Steps 

SAP DRC Cloud Edition is hosted on SAP BTP and connect backend ERP (S/4HANA Cloud, S/4HANA On-Prem) to local tax/government authorities .

Pre-requisites

  1. BTP Global Account CPEA or Pay-as-you-go
  2. Subaccount with Entitlement to SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, Cloud Edition

Activation Steps :

you can run BTP Booster for automatic setup or use below Manual Setup .

1. Entitle the Service in BTP Cockpit

  • Go to your BTP Global Account
  • Navigate to the Subaccount you want to use
  • Open Entitlements Configure Entitlements
  • Add Service Plan: SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, Cloud Edition
  • Select default or the appropriate service plan .

2. Create Service Instance and Service Key

  • Go to Instances and Subscriptions in your BTP Subaccount

 

Click Create → Service Instance

Choose: SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, Cloud Edition

Choose the plan (default) , Set a name

  • After creation, Create Service Key

You’ll use the key (credentials, endpoint) to connect your ERP .

3. Setup Trust & Connectivity

  • If ERP is in S/4HANA Public Cloud: no middleware needed
  • For On-Premise or Private Cloud, set up:

 

-SAP Cloud Connector (to connect backend)

-Trust relationship with SAP Identity Authentication (IAS) if needed

-SAP Integration Suite if required by the scenario

4. Configure Communication Arrangement in ERP

In S/4HANA Cloud:

Use SSCUI or CBC to activate relevant scope items (e.g., 5XU, 5XN, etc.)

Create Communication Arrangements to connect ERP to DRC Cloud via APIs

In S/4HANA On-Premise:

Use SOAMANAGER or manually set up RFC destinations / HTTP connections

-Activate required BC Sets and Business Functions .

Looking forward to hearing you step by step configuration and BTP setup.

Thanks,

Ashish

entry.title: How to Retrigger or correct I-9 - Onboarding
entry.content:
ealing with compliance in SAP SuccessFactors is usually straightforward—until a New Hire enters their Social Security Number or personal details incorrectly on their US Form I-9.🤦‍

If you've tried using the standard "Restart Compliance Process" object, you’ve likely hit a wall: it supports almost all compliance forms except the I-9.

So, how do you fix an I-9 error without deleting the entire onboarding process? The secret lies in a specific object: Trigger I-9 Flow.

✨Step-by-Step

Step 1: Initiating the New Flow

  1. Navigate to Manage Data > Create New.

  2. Select the object Trigger I-9 Flow.

  3. User: Enter the employee’s name.

  4. Trigger Type: Select the appropriate type (e.g., I-9 Reverification or Section 1).

  5. Process ID: Enter the ID of the onbaordee.

  6. Save.

A new task will automatically be assigned to the employee or the designated I-9 Responsible user.

 

✨Now that you’ve started a new flow, you’ll have an old, incorrect task lingering in the system. To avoid confusion and data duplication, you must cancel it:

Step 2: Deleting previous Process

  1. Go to Manage Data and search for Compliance Process.

  2. Locate the specific record using the Compliance Process ID or User ID.

  3. Under Take Action, select Make Correction.

  4. Change the Compliance Process Status to Cancelled.

  5. Save.

entry.title: Re: Sales Order creation via EDI using API - New Customer...
entry.content:
Yes, this is expected behavior when CMIR is missing. SAP does not provide a standard way to “fix” TATX lines. The best practice is to stop them at source (middleware/API) or provide a custom reprocessing path for CSRs.

Enhance your middleware or API mapping logic to check if the buyer part number exists in CMIR before creating the order. If missing, route the order to an error queue instead of creating a TATX line. This way, CSR can fix the CMIR and reprocess the inbound message.

The other option is to use BAdIs like SD_SALES_DOCUMENT_CREATE to intercept order creation. If a buyer part number is found without CMIR, raise an error or park the order rather than creating TATX. This prevents “useless” lines from entering the system.

entry.title: Re: Co-Product Cost Estimate Not Matching Apportionment S...
entry.content:
Why the system values differ is because of the following:

1) Apportionment structure
You have defined 60% for header material 31000001 and 40% for co‑product 31000005.

2) System calculation
It gave 184 vs. 136, which is a 57.5% / 42.5% split — not your maintained percentages.

3) Reason
In joint production, the system considers not only the apportionment structure but also factors like lot size, equivalence numbers, and valuation settings. If equivalence numbers or lot sizes are not aligned with your apportionment percentages, the distribution will deviate.

Having said that, the system is behaving as designed, but the deviation suggests either equivalence numbers or lot size are not aligned with your intended percentages. Adjusting those should bring the distribution back to 192/128.

entry.title: Re: VS Code: Command MDK: Open Mobile App Editor not foun...
entry.content:
Hi @aelsiar,

I've seen this happen after recent updates to the MDK VS Code Extension. The mdk:openMobileAppEditor command is sometimes hidden from the Command Palette if the extension hasn't 'seen' an MDK file yet.

Quick fix:

  • Open your Application.app file.

  • Instead of the Palette, right-click the file in the Explorer and look for 'MDK: Open Editor'.

  • Also, check if the MDK Activity Bar icon is visible on the left. Opening the editor through that view is now the preferred 'Clean Core' workflow for SAP Mobile Services.

In my research on Smart Ports, we rely heavily on these editors to build real-time asset tracking screens—if the UI doesn't activate, usually it's because the MDKProject.json is missing from the root directory!"

Are you working on a project migrated from the SAP Web IDE, or was this created directly within VS Code using the MDK template?

entry.title: Re: Split Valuation with Automatic Batch (Category ‘X’) –...
entry.content: Always specify the batch when creating STOs for materials with valuation category ‘X’. If operationally you don’t know the batch at STO creation, consider redesigning the process to use batch determination so the system can assign it automatically. Without batch, the STO cannot resolve valuation type, so this is expected behavior rather than a bug.

entry.title: Re: How to Retrigger or correct I-9 - Onboarding
entry.content:
Thank you, @FrancoTornaghi155! This is a lifesaver for many admins.

From my perspective in Industrial Systems, handling the I-9 retrigger via Manage Data instead of a full 'Process Restart' is the ultimate 'Clean Core' move. It respects the existing onboarding structure while surgically fixing the compliance error.

A quick tip for Step 1: When selecting the Trigger Type, ensure you distinguish between Section 1 (for employee corrections) and Reverification (for expiring documents). Using the wrong trigger type can lead to the wrong task appearing in the employee's dashboard.

Question: Have you found that this method automatically updates the E-Verify status, or do you still find it necessary to manually trigger a new E-Verify case once Section 2 of the new I-9 flow is completed?

entry.title: Re: SAP DRC Configuration
entry.content:
Great summary, @Ashish13! You’ve captured the essential BTP prerequisites perfectly. From an Industrial Engineering perspective, the transition to SAP DRC is a 'mechanical necessity' for global trade. For the KSA (ZATCA) scenario you mentioned, the integration often hinges on the correct handling of Master Data—specifically ensuring that the cryptographic stamp onboarding is performed correctly in the DRC Cloud Edition app before the first transmission.

One technical addition for your configuration steps:

When setting up Step 4 (Communication Arrangements) in S/4HANA Cloud, don't forget to check the specific Company Code Settings for DRC. You must link your local reporting entity to the country-specific reporting category to ensure the data maps correctly from your SD/FI postings to the legal XML.

Quick question: For the KSA implementation, are you planning to use the standard eDocument Cockpit for manual monitoring, or are you looking to automate the submission process entirely via background jobs?

entry.title: Re: Co-Product Cost Estimate Not Matching Apportionment S...
entry.content:
Hi @Krishna2198,

This is a classic 'Equivalence vs. Percentage' conflict in Controlling (CO). While you've maintained the structure as 60/40, the system calculates the split based on the Equivalence Numbers derived during the costing run.

From an Industrial Systems Engineering perspective, this 57.5/42.5 split suggests the system is adjusting for a specific variable—most likely Lot Size or Rounding in the Equivalence Number calculation.

To fix this:

  • Go to the Production Version of material 31000001 and explicitly check the 'Apportionment Structure' button. Ensure the Equivalence Numbers are set to exactly 6 for the header and 4 for the co-product.

  • Check if there are any Direct Costs in your BOM that are marked as specifically belonging to the co-product; these bypass the apportionment structure entirely.

In my research on Maritime Logistics, we treat these splits like 'Payload Distributions'—if the base units don't align, the final balance will always shift!

entry.title: Re: I am trying to run the inventory audit report but non...
entry.content:
Hi David,

This is standard behavior in SAP Business One: Drop Ship warehouses are non-stock locations used only for logistics, so even if a GRPO is posted to them, the system accepts the document but does not update inventory quantities or values and does not create any valuation entries. That’s why the received units don’t appear in the Item Master Data and why the warehouse is not available in inventory reports like the Inventory Audit Report.

entry.title: Re: BiWhy Jobs – Make Jobs Work for You, Not Against You
entry.content:
This is an eye-opening post, Dilya! In Industrial Engineering, we often talk about 'mechanical failure,' but your analysis of 172 days of wasted processing time shows that 'digital failure' can be just as costly in real compute terms.

I especially like the History Analytics approach of defining a failure rate. It moves the admin from a 'reactive' state—waiting for user complaints—to a 'predictive' one.

Quick question: When you identify these high-cost, failing reports, have you found a specific 'Clean Core' method for communicating with the report owners to decommission them without causing disruption to their perceived business needs?

entry.content:
This is a fantastic addition to the ABAP Development toolkit, @uladzislau_pralat!

In my research on Smart Ports, we frequently have to trace custom analytics back to base tables like VBAP to ensure our digital twins are pulling accurate data. The standard ADT analyzer can sometimes be overwhelming with its deep association trees, so your 'one-level-at-a-time' approach is exactly what's needed for focused debugging.

entry.title: Re: Sales Organization address fields in in Email Templates
entry.content:
This is a very helpful clarification from the SAP Support team!

From an Industrial Systems perspective, having the correct Sales Org address on outbound emails is vital for regional compliance and clear communication with port authorities and partners. The path _SalesArea._SalesOrganization._Address is the standard 'Clean Core' way to access these master data attributes.

Pro Tip: If you find yourself needing these fields across multiple templates, consider creating a Custom CDS View that joins the Sales Org address data to your primary document types. This can sometimes make the field selection process even faster for future template projects.

Quick question: Are you planning to use these address fields for a specific regional legal requirement, like the KSA ZATCA invoicing regulations, or is this primarily for general corporate branding?

entry.title: Re: The Quiet Revolution in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud Logistics: Clean Core, Transparency, and AI
entry.content:
Hi @Melih,

Thank you for the thoughtful perspective. You raise a fair point about legacy systems—they have been the robust backbone of supply chains for decades, and my intention isn't to dismiss their immense capabilities.

My reference to "messy backend modifications" is specifically regarding the long-term technical debt that historically accumulated in traditional on-premise environments. Often, what started as "light customization" compounded over years of upgrades, creating complex maintenance challenges. In the context of S/4HANA Public Cloud, the strict governance around Key User Extensibility and specific BAdIs acts as a safeguard. It enforces a discipline that ensures our enhancements remain upgrade-safe and resilient by design, shifting us away from the highly flexible but sometimes perilous enhancement frameworks of the past.

I'd be interested to hear how your teams are navigating the transition to this more strictly governed extensibility model.

entry.title: Re: When One Release Has to Reach Many SAP Cloud Integration Tenants
entry.content:
Hi @dougliu,

This is actually a very real problem, especially after go-live when clients operate across multiple tenants (DEV, QA, PRD). From my experience, once things scale, it becomes difficult to keep visibility across tenants, especially when trying to understand what’s different or out of sync.

The orchestration layer you described makes a lot of sense from a delivery perspective. One gap I’ve consistently seen, though, is around comparison and drift visibility across tenants. Today, there isn’t an easy or native way to compare artifacts or packages between systems without a lot of manual effort.

I’m currently working on a tool that focuses specifically on that side cross-tenant comparison at the package level to highlight differences and help teams quickly identify drift between environments.

Curious how others are currently handling this, or if they’re just relying on manual checks.

entry.title: Re: Billing Document created but no eDocument appears in...
entry.content:
Hello!

 

An eDocument is supposed to be created automatically, but only if specific country and master‑data conditions are met.

In SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud, an eDocument instance is created automatically at the moment the billing document is posted, only when the billing document qualifies for a country‑specific e‑invoicing scenario delivered by SAP.

If those conditions are not met, the billing document is still posted successfully, but no eDocument is created at all. In that case, nothing appears in:

  • Manage Electronic Documents (F4306)
  • eDocument Cockpit (EDOC_COCKPIT)

This is standard SAP behavior and explains why there are no records or error messages for the invoice.

 

The Central Business Configuration (CBC) activities you completed are necessary, but they are only technical enablers. They do not by themselves trigger eDocument creation. For an eDocument to be created, SAP also evaluates:

  • The company code country
  • Whether that country has SAP‑delivered e‑invoicing localization
  • Whether company and customer master data meet the country‑specific requirements

If any of these conditions are not satisfied, the document is considered out of scope for eDocument processing and is skipped silently.

 

In the vast majority of similar cases, the root cause is missing or incorrect country‑specific identification data, specifically:

Company Code:

  • Must belong to a country supported by SAP e‑invoicing localization
  • Must have the required Party Identification Type (for example, VAT) assigned

Customer (Business Partner)

  • Must have a valid country‑specific identification (for example, VAT number)
  • The identification type must match what SAP expects for that country

If either the company or the customer does not have the correct party ID maintained, SAP will not create an eDocument, even though:

  • The billing document is posted successfully
  • CBC configuration is complete
  • eDocument types are assigned in CBC

 

In SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud:

  • If an eDocument is created, errors are visible in Error Analysis
  • If an eDocument is not eligible, no record is created at all

That is why no errors are shown, no logs are visible - the document is absent from the eDocument app.

To fix the issue is recommended to validate the followings:

  1. Company code
    • Country is supported for SAP e‑invoicing
    • Correct party identification type is assigned
  1. Customer master data
  • Valid country‑specific VAT / registration number maintained
  • Correct identification type used
  1. Billing document
  • Billing type is mapped to the correct country‑specific eDocument type

 

Additional documentation:

  • At the following link you will find a SAP course about the Creating Electronic Document and Legal Report:

https://learning.sap.com/courses/applying-localization-as-a-self-service-for-sap-s-4hana-cloud-public-edition/creating-electronic-document-and-legal-report

  • At the following link you will find a similar question addressed on SAP Community:

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-q-a/no-edocument-created-in-ecockpit-sap-public-cloud/qaq-p/14278671

entry.title: Re: Port to port (PP) or Port to Door (PD) for sales sea...
entry.content:
Hello!

The automatic creation of Sea Freight Bookings (SFB) from a Sales Order → Freight Unit (FU) follows standard TM stage‑building logic. When an SFB is created automatically from an FU, the system determines the movement type (DD, DP, PD, PP) based on:

  • Incoterms
  • Incoterm Location
  • Shipping point
  • Ship‑from / Ship‑to locations coming from the Sales Order

If the Sales Order has a shipping point at the origin, TM assumes a Door‑based start, which results in:

  • DD (Door‑to‑Door) or DP (Door‑to‑Port) movement types
  • Origin stage starting at the Shipping Point, not at a Port

For Port‑to‑Port (PP) or Port‑to‑Door (PD) movement types, TM expects the first logistical location to be a Port, not a Shipping Point. In Public Cloud the Sales Order represents a physical ship‑from location.

As long as a Shipping Point exists in the Sales Order:

  • TM will interpret the origin as a door
  • Automatic stage construction will not start at a port
  • PP / PD cannot be derived automatically from the predecessor document

The observation is correct and aligned with standard logic.

When:

  1. Create the SFB manually
  2. Select PP or PD as the movement type
  3. Assign the Freight Unit

The system:

  • Defaults the Port of Loading using the Shipping Point as a reference
  • Builds stages according to the selected movement type

This is expected behavior and does not indicate a configuration issue.

About the current workaround:

  • Create SFB manually
  • Select PP or PD
  • Assign the FU
  • Add or adjust an intermediate stage to correctly define the Loading Port

This workaround is valid and can’t be influenced automatically in Public Edition because automatic PP / PD determination cannot be controlled from: Sales Order fields, incoterms alone, standard CBC configuration. TM always trusts the Shipping Point as the initial origin.

Therefore automatic SFB creation is designed primarily for door‑based scenarios, a port‑first scenarios require manual intervention.

Recommended approach going forward for sea freight scenarios where transportation responsibility starts at the port:

  • Continue using manual SFB creation for these scenarios
  • Select the correct movement type (PP / PD)
  • Assign the Freight Unit
  • Let TM determine ports correctly from the movement type

When an SFB is created automatically from a Sales Order → Freight Unit (FU):

  • TM builds stages based on Sales Order logistics data
  • The Shipping Point is treated as the physical start location
  • Incoterms and Incoterm Location influence stage building, not the movement type
  • As a result, TM defaults to door‑based movement types such as:
  • • Door‑to‑Door (DD)
  • • Door‑to‑Port (DP)

Port‑to‑Port (PP) or Port‑to‑Door (PD) scenarios cannot be derived automatically because TM expects the transportation responsibility to start at the port, not at a physical shipping location.

In the Public Cloud the Sales Order always represents a physical ship‑from location and there is no Sales Order field or configuration that tells TM to ignore the Shipping Point or start transportation directly at a Port.

When the SFB is created manually (via Manage Ocean Freight Bookings or Transportation Cockpit) the planner explicitly selects the movement type (PP or PD). TM then builds stages according to that movement type and correctly determines Port of Loading and Port of Discharge. Freight Units can be assigned afterward without changing the movement logic.

 

Additional link references below:

https://learning.sap.com/courses/implementing-sap-s-4hana-cloud-public-edition-transportation-management/creating-freight-bookings_d63cea76-a293-4852-ab88-cf872c1f00e2

https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-q-a/how-to-create-freight-order-freight-booking-freight-unit-in-s4-public-cloud/qaq-p/12790551

entry.title: Re: Using BAS to create an ALP chart does not generate di...
entry.content:
Hello!

In SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition, a field used as a chart dimension must be explicitly marked as a dimension at the analytical level. Defining the field only in @ui.chart.dimensions and @ui.dimensionAttributes is not sufficient.

If the field is not analytically classified as a dimension, the Fiori analytical engine ignores it for grouping, generates a chart with measures only and leaves the dimension area empty (what you are seeing).

Details here:

“For a tabular display of the query result, the elements can be positioned on multiple axes for the default layout. The elements can be directly annotated with their axis. Measures (elements that can be aggregated (@Aggregation.default)) all need to be on the same axis. The annotation of the first measure will therefore be used for all measures of the query. If no AnalyticsDetails.query.axis is found, the system positions the measures on the columns.”

https://help.sap.com/doc/abapdocu_cp_index_htm/CLOUD/en-US/ABENCDS_741079388-_ANNO.html

 

The issue is not with the chart syntax itself. The missing part is the analytical annotation on the field used as a dimension.

What happens in this case "supplier" is defined as a normal element and used in @ui.chart.dimensions. But not declared as an analytical dimension. As a result chart renders, measure is shown, no grouping - no dimension displayed.

Correct and required solution is to mark the dimension field with @AnalyticsDetails.query.axis: #ROWS (or #COLUMNS, depending on the chart design).

In S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition:

  • CDS analytical queries are strictly validated
  • UI annotations do not implicitly define analytics behavior
  • The analytical engine only considers fields assigned to ROWS, COLUMNS, FREE axes

If a field is not assigned to an axis is treated as a technical attribute and cannot appear as a chart dimension.

Additional checks to avoid similar issues, also ensure:

  1. The dimension field:
  • Is not aggregated
  • Is not calculated at runtime in an unsupported way
  1. Measures have @DefaultAggregation
  2. The CDS entity is declared with @analytics.query: true
  3. You are using a supported analytical chart type (COLUMN, BAR, LINE, etc.)

 

Additional details here:

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/step-2-define-the-analytical-query-cds-view/ba-p/13349493

https://learning.sap.com/courses/discovering-sap-s-4hana-embedded-analytics/creating-analytical-queries_f19d294b-e1bf-4549-846f-340f695663f7

https://help.sap.com/docs/SUPPORT_CONTENT/abapcdsaq/3361385699.html?locale=en-US

entry.title: Re: Dimension Hierarchy Text gets truncated in SAC stories
entry.content:
Dear pjshah,

Hope you are well. Thank you for your message.

To address this scenario effectively, it is recommended reviewing the following points to ensure a resolution:

1. In this widget (such as bar chart) Styling panel, please kindly check which option has been selected under "Axis Label Truncation". Please kindly try changing the selected option for "Axis Label Truncation" and check whether this issue would still occur.

2. Please kindly check whether the axis label behaviour would be consistent under the story Edit Mode, Full Viewer Mode as well as Lite Viewer Mode. 

3. When accessing the story in browser, please kindly check whether the browser zoom level is 100%. If not, please kindly change the browser zoom level to 100% and check whether this issue would still occur.

4. It is also recommended to collect the har file tracing the process of refreshing this widget. Then, in related getResponse call, please kindly check whether the proper axis label text has been returned from backend. If yes, this issue may need to further investigate from SAC perspective. If not, this issue may need further investigate from BW perspective.

Thank you for your time.

Best wishes,

Lena

entry.title: Re: Managing Mixed Fiscal Year Variants on SAP Cloud ERP
entry.content:
Hello!

 

In SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition, different fiscal year variants can’t be assigned per company code in the leading ledger. The SAP supported and clean core solution for mixed fiscal calendars is to keep the leading ledger on a common fiscal year (usually calendar year) and use an alternative fiscal year variant assigned to a non‑leading ledger for company codes that require a different fiscal calendar. This approach is explicitly documented by SAP.

Source link here:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0fa84c9d9c634132b7c4abb9ffdd8f06/9dda2d38e0e74490a99dcaf5b5c678f3.html?locale=en-US&q=Alternative+Fiscal+Year+Variant

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/3e5fcf2c768746049b5627bd5a42f720/98d305b8e50e4772bca32f1b2ef724ea.html?locale=en-US&q=Alternative+Fiscal+Year+Variant

 

Based on SAP’s official documentation on Alternative Fiscal Year Variants, the following design is the recommended and fully supported approach in SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition.

Only one fiscal year variant can be used for the entire system at leading‑ledger level. This fiscal year variant is defined during Central Business Configuration (CBC) in the activity Specify Primary Finance Settings. Once activated applies to all company codes, to all ledgers and can’t be changed later.

This means it is not possible to assign different fiscal year variants directly to different company codes in the leading ledger (0L).

 

In practice, this means:

  1. Leading Ledger (0L)
  • Fiscal Year Variant: Calendar Year (for example, K4 – January to December)
  • Purpose: Group reporting and consolidation, standard financial reporting, core operational accounting processes
  1. Non‑Leading Ledger (for example, 2L or 3L)
  • Fiscal Year Variant: Academic / non‑calendar year (for example, September to August)
  • Purpose: local statutory reporting, local management or regulatory requirements for specific company codes

SAP explicitly states that alternative fiscal year variants are designed exactly for this purpose and must be assigned to non‑leading ledgers and specific company codes.

 

Key Prerequisites according to SAP Help:

  • Parallel accounting must be set up (leading + non‑leading ledger)
  • The affected company codes must be assigned to the non‑leading ledger
  • No postings must exist yet for those company codes when the alternative fiscal year variant is assigned

 

SAP also clarifies the implications of this setup:

  • Fiscal years can be open in one ledger and closed in another
  • Period‑end and year‑end activities may need to be executed per ledger
  • Leading‑ledger processes (Group Reporting, CO, core closing) continue to follow the calendar year
  • Local reporting can follow the academic year via the non‑leading ledger
  • This behavior is expected and supported, not a workaround.

 

A separate tenant is only required if:

  • The academic/non‑calendar year must be the leading fiscal year
  • Core operational processes (closing, asset life, budgeting, controlling) must fully follow the academic cycle
  • A calendar‑year leading ledger is not acceptable

If the academic year is required only for local or statutory reporting, SAP explicitly recommends one tenant with parallel ledgers instead of multiple tenants.

entry.title: Re: Feasibility Request – MRP Behavior for Reservations a...
entry.content:
Hello!

Requirement 1: Exclude open reservations from “available stock” for MRP in standard Public Cloud is not supported. Reservations are intended to be taken into account by MRP to ensure material availability.

SAP describes reservations as a mechanism that ensures material availability at the required time, and is taken into account by MRP so procurement happens in time if stock is insufficient. In standard MRP logic, reservations are part of demand/requirements and will reduce the net available quantity for planning purposes. The intended behavior is that reservations are MRP relevant demand.

Practical implication for mentioned example:

  • Stock 100
  • Open reservations 20

MRP will treat this as demand and will plan accordingly.

While MRP cannot be instructed to “ignore reservations”, SAP provides availability check behavior at reservation creation time (ATP/availability check scope), which helps control whether a reservation can be confirmed when stock is missing.

 

Requirement 2: Exclude unapproved PRs from incoming supply in MRP not supported as an MRP netting rule.

SAP describes purchase requisitions as standard MRP procurement proposals / elements (receipts) used by MRP to cover shortages.

SAP also notes that PRs are internal planning/procurement elements that exist to trigger purchasing action and can be created automatically by MRP.

SAP provides Flexible Workflow for Purchase Requisitions, which supports multi-step approvals (overall release or item release). Approvers process PRs via My Inbox, and workflows are configured in Manage Workflows for Purchase Requisitions.

If the core goal is “don’t let unapproved PRs drive procurement execution”, SAP supports that through: PR workflow approval steps (overall/item release) and keeping PR conversion/processing under control via the approval status (business process). This prevents buyers from acting on PRs prematurely, but it does not force MRP to ignore them in netting.

 

Links here:

https://learning.sap.com/courses/exploring-production-planning-in-sap-s-4hana/understanding-mrp-in-sap-s-4hana

 

https://learning.sap.com/courses/configuring-supply-chain-business-scenarios-in-sap-s4hana-cloud-public-edition/introducing-basic-available-to-promise_ee3a33eb-4a91-4f85-a8b6-9bc1189e9540

 

https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-q-a/mrp-execution-without-consideration-of-unapproved-prs-and-pos/qaq-p/9779619

 

https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-blog-posts-by-sap/sourcing-amp-procurement-in-sap-s-4hana-cloud-public-edition-2508/ba-p/14175706

entry.title: Re: Validation before putaway while creating task for Aut...
entry.content:
Good day @Harry94 

Thank you for your question.

The object you mentioned is a classic EWM enhancement object which is under the /SWCM/. However, identifying an alternative enhancement point or BAdI for custom bin filtering would be an EWM ABAP enhancement topic. This type of enhancement is available in an on-prem/private cloud environment where you have the possibility to carry out ABAP enhancement implementations.

So, in this case, the Public Cloud does not offer you a standard Fiori configuration or key user extensibility. In a standard cloud setup, you should check custom bin filtering against standard EWM configuration options, e.g., storage section search, bin type determination, putaway control indicator, etc.

So, if your requirement is to inject custom ABAP logic into automatic bin determination, then consider on-prem or private cloud.

 

Best regards

Chris
